figures.name: Viracocha
figures.gloss: Wiraqucha, the maker who rose from Lake Titicaca, shaped a first humankind from stone, broke it, made it again, then walked north as a wandering teacher and vanished over the western sea — promising to return.
figures.facet: The creator who leaves a forwarding address: most withdrawn makers simply recede, but Viracocha exits across the water as a recognisable traveller-teacher, so that the sails of Pizarro could be mistaken for his promised return — a cosmology with a fatal open door.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from colonial chroniclers (Betanzos, Cieza de León, Molina, Sarmiento); the pre-conquest Viracocha is overlaid with Christianised 'creator-god' framing imposed by Spanish recorders.

figures.name: Inti
figures.gloss: The Sun, divine father of the Inca royal line and patron of the empire, whose great festival Inti Raymi crowned the Andean year; the Sapa Inca ruled as his living son and the Coricancha was sheathed in his gold.
figures.facet: Sun as the charter of a dynasty: where many solar gods light the world for all, Inti is above all the *ancestor of the kings* — sovereignty itself descends from the solar disk, making astronomy a title-deed.
figures.provenance: Inti's pre-eminence partly reflects Inca state cult elevating a dynastic sun over older regional deities; the relation of Inti to Viracocha is debated in the sources.

figures.name: Mama Killa
figures.gloss: Mama Quilla, 'Mother Moon', sister-wife of Inti, mistress of the calendar, of festivals and of women; her image was silver as Inti's was gold, and eclipses were a beast devouring her, beaten back by noise.
figures.facet: The moon as wife AND sister of the sun, both his equal and his consort — the lunar reckoner of time given a queenly, maternal face rather than a wandering or punished one.

figures.name: Pachamama
figures.gloss: Mother of earth and time together — *pacha* means both world and moment — the fertile ground who must be fed before she gives, honoured to this day with the buried offering of the despacho and the first sip poured to the soil.
figures.facet: Earth and time as one mother: Pachamama is not only the soil but the unfolding world-age itself, so to tend the ground is to keep the order of the era — a great-mother who is also the measure of the world's seasons.
figures.provenance: Still actively venerated across the Andes; the living folk-cult preserves a layer older and more continuous than the colonial chronicles.

figures.name: Illapa
figures.gloss: Thunder, lightning and rain personified — a warrior who crosses the sky drawing water from the celestial river (the Milky Way) in his jug and shattering it with a crack of his sling, the flash his garment and the bolt his stone.
figures.facet: The thunderbolt as a slung stone: Illapa fights not with hammer or axe but with the Andean herder's own weapon, the sling — the storm-god dressed in the technology of the people who feared him.

figures.name: Pariacaca
figures.gloss: Born as five eggs that hatched into five falcons and then men, the storm-and-water hero of the Huarochirí highlands who loosed a flood to drown the false fire-god Huallallo Carhuincho and ordered the irrigation of the valleys.
figures.facet: The shard that kept its own scripture: Pariacaca survives in the Huarochirí Manuscript, the one extended mythology written down in Quechua by Andean hands — a flood-bringing rival storm-power outside the Cuzco state cult.
figures.provenance: From the early-17th-c. Huarochirí Manuscript, the closest thing to an indigenous Andean sacred text, compiled under Francisco de Ávila's extirpation campaign yet recording local Yauyos tradition.

figures.name: Mama Cocha
figures.gloss: Mama Qucha, 'Mother Sea', the great waters from whom fish and rain ultimately come; coastal peoples implored her for calm and for the shoals, and the highland lakes were her inland faces.
figures.facet: The sea revered by a mountain empire: an Andean state whose heart was the high sierra still kept the Mother of Waters at the centre of its cosmos, the western ocean over which Viracocha departed.

figures.name: Inkarrí
figures.gloss: Inka Rey, the dismembered king of post-conquest Andean prophecy: the Spaniards severed and buried his head, but the head grows its body back beneath the earth, and when it is whole the Inca order — *pachakuti*, the world's overturning — will return.
figures.facet: Resurrection rewritten as resistance: born after the conquest, Inkarrí turns the dying-and-rising pattern into a buried promise of historical reversal — the murdered sovereign regrowing underground as the hope of a colonised people.
figures.provenance: A 20th-c. ethnographic myth (Arguedas and others) of the post-conquest Andes, not a pre-Columbian deity; it fuses Inca memory, Christian resurrection imagery and millenarian hope — flagged as syncretic by formation.

figures.name: Supay
figures.gloss: Lord of the *Ukhu Pacha*, the inner/lower world and its dead, and of the mineral wealth and dangers within the mountain; the miners of the Andes still placate him as 'El Tío' deep in the silver and tin shafts.
figures.facet: The demonised sovereign who never left the mine: like other underworld lords flattened into a 'devil' by missionaries, Supay alone keeps a living, fed cult underground — propitiated with coca and liquor by the very Catholics who renamed him.
figures.provenance: Pre-Christian Supay was the morally-neutral lord of the below; colonial usage forced the word to translate the Christian Devil, and the modern miners' 'Tío' is the surviving older figure beneath that overlay.

edges.note: Creator and royal Sun stand at the head of the Andean cosmos together; the chronicles dispute their precedence — Viracocha the maker, Inti the dynastic patron the maker set in the sky.
Gold Sun and silver Moon, husband-and-sister, the imperial divine couple whose images faced each other in the Coricancha and whose festivals divided the Inca year.
Mother Earth and Mother Sea, the two great female waters-and-ground of the Andean world, jointly invoked as the givers of all sustenance.
Two Andean masters of storm and water — the Cuzco state's Illapa and the Huarochirí highlands' flood-loosing Pariacaca — the same rain-and-lightning power in imperial and local dress.
Lord of the inner world below and Mother of the surface earth: the Andean three-world scheme (Hanan/Kay/Ukhu Pacha) sets Supay's depths against Pachamama's fertile ground.

Two American creator-teachers who civilise, then depart over the sea promising return — and whose myths each opened a door the conquistadors walked through; a striking structural twin across unrelated families.
Both stand as the remote, self-existent maker behind the working gods of their pantheon; a loose parallel of the high creator-principle, with no contact or descent.
Sun-gods who father kings: as Inti sires the Sapa Inca, Ra is the dynastic father of pharaohs — solar sovereignty independently arrived at in the Andes and the Nile.
The sun as royal ancestor of a sacred dynasty — the Sapa Inca descends from Inti as the Japanese emperor from Amaterasu, the same solar charter of kingship in two unconnected worlds.
Solar deities at the head of a sky-cult; both fountains of royal legitimacy, though Inti is a dynastic ancestor where Sūrya is the wheeling celestial eye.
Two American solar patrons of an empire whose state cult fed the sun to keep the world turning — Inti through Inti Raymi, Huitzilopochtli through war and offering.
Moon and sea bound by the tides within the Andean cosmos; both 'mothers' (mama) of the watery, cyclical order beneath Inti's daylight realm.
Reckoners of the calendar by the moon's course; Mama Killa keeps the festivals where the Baltic Mėnulis keeps the marriage-myth — parallel lunar timekeepers, no shared line.
Earth herself as mother and source, fed and thanked by those who till her; Pachamama and Gaia are independent personifications of the living ground.
American earth-mothers who both give and take life — Pachamama demanding her offering before she yields, Coatlicue devouring as she bears; the ambivalent ground of two cordilleras.
Grain-and-ground mothers whose first-fruits and libations secure the harvest; the Andean despacho echoes Demeter's agrarian rite without any descent.
Two American rain-and-lightning lords on whom the farmed highlands depended; both draw and break the celestial waters, fearsome bringers of the planting rains.
Warrior storm-gods who release the held-up waters with a thunder-stroke; Illapa shatters his celestial jug as Indra breaks Vṛtra's grip on the rivers — same template, separate worlds.
Thunder-warriors whose hurled weapon is the lightning — Illapa's slung stone for Thor's hammer; the storm-wielder pattern arrived at far outside Indo-European.
Both hurl the thunderbolt as a stone from the sky — Illapa's sling-stone and Sango's celt — independent witnesses to lightning imagined as a flung rock.

Both order the Andean landscape and waters as culture-bringers — Viracocha the wandering teacher, Pariacaca the flood-loosing hero who lays out the irrigation; the highland and imperial faces of the same shaping power.
Hero born in unusual form (Pariacaca from five eggs, Māui prematurely cast to the sea) who reshapes the world for humankind; a loose culture-hero rhyme across the Pacific, flagged as a reach.
Both primal mother-bodies of the world, but Mama Cocha is the sea where Gaia is the earth — a divergent parallel, the maternal deep against the maternal ground.

Inkarrí's buried head that regrows its body and will rise to overturn the world is built directly upon the resurrection preached by the conquerors — a colonial fusion of Inca memory and Christian rising.
Dismembered sovereigns who regenerate from the buried body — Osiris reassembled in the underworld, Inkarrí regrowing beneath the earth; the murdered king as the seed of return.
Both carry the promise of a departed/defeated lord's return that becomes a charge against the conqueror; Inkarrí's underground resurrection rhymes loosely with the awaited Feathered Serpent.
Sovereigns of the world below who are also keepers of its buried wealth — Supay of the mineral veins, Hades the 'rich one' — the underworld as the storehouse of riches.
American lords of the lowest world and its dead; Supay's Ukhu Pacha answers Mictlantecuhtli's Mictlan, each the rightful master of the realm beneath.

Underworld lords of wealth and the dead alike demoted by Christian missionaries into 'the Devil', yet each keeping an older, richer face beneath the slander — a shared scar of conversion across families.
Both belong to the high creative order of their cosmos, but the link is faint — Viracocha the personal maker, Ranginui the sky-parent; included as a weak creator-principle echo only.
